valid-anagram.intuition: The problem asks to check if two strings are anagrams, which implies comparing character frequencies. steps.detail: Iterate through the second string and decrement the count of each character in the hash table.

lfu-cache.intuition: To implement an LFU cache, we need to efficiently track both the frequency of access and the recency of use for each item. A combination of a hash map for O(1) access and a doubly linked list (or multiple linked lists) to maintain order based on frequency is crucial for meeting the performance requirements.
lfu-cache.pattern_connection: This problem leverages the Linked List pattern to manage the order of elements based on their usage frequency. The hash map acts as an index into the linked list structure, enabling fast lookups.

steps.title: Data Structures
steps.detail: Use a HashMap to store key-value pairs and a Doubly Linked List (or a map of Doubly Linked Lists) to manage nodes based on their frequency.

steps.title: Node Structure
steps.detail: Each node in the linked list should store the key, value, its frequency count, and pointers to the previous and next nodes.

steps.title: Get Operation
steps.detail: Retrieve the value for a key, update its frequency, and move the node to the appropriate frequency list (or update its position within a frequency list).

steps.title: Put Operation
steps.detail: If the key exists, update its value and frequency. If not, add a new node. If the cache is full, evict the least frequently used item (and among those, the least recently used).

steps.title: Frequency Management
steps.detail: Maintain a separate linked list for each frequency count, or a single linked list where nodes are ordered by frequency and then by recency.

steps.title: Eviction Policy
steps.detail: When capacity is reached, remove the node from the front of the least frequent list (or the node with the lowest frequency and oldest timestamp).

edge_cases: Cache is full and a new element needs to be added.
Multiple elements have the same minimum frequency.
Getting a key that does not exist.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach might be simpler to conceptualize but can lead to O(log n) complexity for updates due to PriorityQueue operations, failing the O(1) requirement.

alternatives.approach: Two HashMaps and Doubly Linked Lists
alternatives.tradeoff: This is the optimal approach, providing O(1) time complexity for both get and put operations by carefully managing frequency lists.

maximum-width-of-binary-tree.intuition: The width of a binary tree at a level is the number of nodes between the leftmost and rightmost non-null nodes at that level, including nulls. To calculate this, we need to assign a unique index to each node based on its position in a complete binary tree and then find the difference between the maximum and minimum indices at each level.
maximum-width-of-binary-tree.pattern_connection: This problem is a variation of Tree traversal, specifically Level Order Traversal (BFS). We augment the standard BFS by tracking node indices to determine the width.

steps.title: Node Indexing
steps.detail: Assign an index to each node. For a node at index `i`, its left child gets `2*i` and its right child gets `2*i + 1`.

steps.title: Level Order Traversal (BFS)
steps.detail: Use a queue to perform a level order traversal. Store pairs of (node, index) in the queue.

steps.title: Track Level Boundaries
steps.detail: For each level, record the index of the first node encountered (minimum index) and the index of the last node encountered (maximum index).

steps.title: Calculate Width
steps.detail: The width of a level is `max_index - min_index + 1`. Keep track of the maximum width found across all levels.

steps.title: Handle Null Nodes
steps.detail: When adding children to the queue, only add non-null children. The indexing scheme implicitly handles the gaps created by null nodes.

steps.title: Integer Overflow
steps.detail: Use `long` for node indices to prevent potential integer overflow, especially for deep or wide trees.

edge_cases: A skewed tree (all left or all right children).
Tree with gaps (null nodes) at various levels.
Very deep or very wide trees that might cause integer overflow for indices.

alternatives.tradeoff: DFS can also be used, but it requires more complex state management to track the first and last node indices at each level, making BFS a more natural fit.

alternatives.approach: Level Order Traversal without explicit indexing
alternatives.tradeoff: Simply counting nodes per level is incorrect as it doesn't account for the positional width defined by nulls.

is-graph-bipartite.intuition: A graph is bipartite if its nodes can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects a node in one set to a node in the other set. This is equivalent to checking if the graph can be colored using only two colors (say, 0 and 1) such that no two adjacent nodes have the same color.
is-graph-bipartite.pattern_connection: This problem falls under the Graph Traversal pattern. We can use either Breadth-First Search (BFS) or Depth-First Search (DFS) to explore the graph and attempt a 2-coloring.

steps.title: Initialization
steps.detail: Create a color array (e.g., `colors`) of the same size as the number of nodes, initialized to a value indicating 'uncolored' (e.g., -1).

steps.title: Iterate Through Nodes
steps.detail: Loop through each node in the graph. If a node is uncolored, start a traversal (BFS or DFS) from it.

steps.title: Start Traversal
steps.detail: Assign the starting node an initial color (e.g., 0). Add it to the queue (for BFS) or call the recursive function (for DFS).

steps.title: Coloring Neighbors
steps.detail: During traversal, for each visited node, iterate through its neighbors. If a neighbor is uncolored, assign it the opposite color of the current node and add it to the queue/call recursively.

steps.title: Conflict Detection
steps.detail: If a neighbor is already colored and has the same color as the current node, the graph is not bipartite. Return `false` immediately.

steps.title: Completion
steps.detail: If the traversal completes for all connected components without any conflicts, the graph is bipartite. Return `true`.

edge_cases: Graph with odd-length cycles (guaranteed not bipartite).

alternatives.tradeoff: DFS can also be used for coloring. The logic is similar, but it uses recursion instead of a queue. Both BFS and DFS have the same time complexity.

Union-Find can be adapted to check for bipartiteness by maintaining sets of nodes that must have the same color and sets that must have different colors. This can be more complex to implement correctly.

steps.title: Copy Second Half

steps.detail: The final count represents the length of the last word.

edge_cases: String has multiple spaces between words.

alternatives.tradeoff: Using `s.trim().split(" ")` is concise but might be less efficient due to the overhead of creating intermediate arrays and strings. It also requires careful handling of multiple spaces.

Iterating from the start requires keeping track of the length of the *current* word and updating a 'last word length' variable whenever a word ends. This is slightly more complex than iterating from the end.

min-cost-climbing-stairs.intuition: We want to find the minimum cost to reach the top of the stairs. Since we can take one or two steps at a time, the minimum cost to reach a particular step depends on the minimum cost to reach the previous one or two steps. This overlapping subproblem structure suggests dynamic programming.
min-cost-climbing-stairs.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic example of 1-D Dynamic Programming. The state `dp[i]` represents the minimum cost to reach step `i`, and the transitions depend on previous states.

steps.title: Define DP State
steps.detail: Let `dp[i]` be the minimum cost to reach step `i`.

steps.title: Base Cases
steps.detail: Initialize `dp[0] = cost[0]` and `dp[1] = cost[1]`, as these are the starting points.

steps.title: Recurrence Relation
steps.detail: For `i >= 2`, the minimum cost to reach step `i` is `cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2])`. This means we pay the cost of the current step plus the minimum cost to reach either the previous step or the step before that.

steps.title: Iteration
steps.detail: Iterate from `i = 2` up to `n` (where `n` is the number of steps, which is `cost.length`). Calculate `dp[i]` using the recurrence relation.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.detail: The final answer is the minimum cost to reach the 'top', which means reaching either step `n-1` or step `n-2` and taking the final step off the stairs. So, the result is `min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2])`.

edge_cases: Cost array with only one element.

alternatives.tradeoff: We only need the previous two DP values to calculate the current one. We can optimize space complexity from O(n) to O(1) by using two variables to store the previous two minimum costs.
A top-down recursive approach with memoization can also solve this, but it might have higher overhead due to function calls and potentially hit recursion depth limits for large inputs.

replace-elements-with-greatest-element-o.intuition: The problem requires replacing each element in an array with the greatest element among the elements to its right. The last element should be replaced with -1. This suggests iterating through the array and keeping track of the maximum value encountered so far from the right.
replace-elements-with-greatest-element-o.pattern_connection: This problem is a variation of Array manipulation. A single pass, typically from right to left, is efficient for problems requiring information about elements to the right of the current position.

steps.title: Initialize Maximum
steps.detail: Initialize a variable `max_so_far` to -1. This will store the greatest element encountered so far from the right.

steps.title: Iterate from Right to Left
steps.detail: Start iterating through the array from the second-to-last element (`n-2`) down to the first element (`0`).

steps.title: Store Current Element
steps.detail: Before updating the current element, store its original value in a temporary variable (e.g., `temp = arr[i]`).

steps.title: Replace Element
steps.detail: Replace the current element `arr[i]` with the current `max_so_far`.

steps.title: Update Maximum
steps.detail: Update `max_so_far` to be the maximum of its current value and the `temp` (original value of `arr[i]`). This ensures `max_so_far` always holds the greatest element seen to the right of the *next* element to be processed.

steps.title: Handle Last Element
steps.detail: The loop starts from `n-2`, so `arr[n-1]` is implicitly handled by the initial `max_so_far = -1`.

edge_cases: Input array has only one element.
All elements in the array are the same.
Array is sorted in ascending order.
Array is sorted in descending order.

alternatives.tradeoff: For each element, iterate through all elements to its right to find the maximum. This results in O(n^2) time complexity, which is less efficient than the O(n) single-pass approach.
A stack could be used, but it doesn't offer a significant advantage over the right-to-left single pass for this specific problem and might add complexity.

is-subsequence.intuition: We need to determine if string `s` is a subsequence of string `t`. A subsequence is formed by deleting zero or more characters from the original string without changing the order of the remaining characters. This implies we need to find the characters of `s` within `t` in the correct order.
is-subsequence.pattern_connection: This problem can be solved using a Two-Pointer approach, which is common for problems involving comparing or traversing sequences (like strings or arrays) in a coordinated manner.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Initialize two pointers: `s_ptr` to 0 (for string `s`) and `t_ptr` to 0 (for string `t`).

steps.title: Iterate Through `t`
steps.detail: Iterate through string `t` using `t_ptr`.

steps.title: Match Characters
steps.detail: If `s[s_ptr]` matches `t[t_ptr]`, it means we've found the next character of the subsequence. Increment `s_ptr`.

steps.title: Advance `t` Pointer
steps.detail: Always increment `t_ptr` in each iteration to move through string `t`.

steps.title: Check Completion
steps.detail: If `s_ptr` reaches the end of string `s` (i.e., `s_ptr == s.length()`), it means all characters of `s` were found in `t` in the correct order, so `s` is a subsequence. Return `true`.

steps.title: End of `t`
steps.detail: If `t_ptr` reaches the end of string `t` but `s_ptr` has not reached the end of `s`, then `s` is not a subsequence. Return `false`.

alternatives.tradeoff: A DP approach can also solve this, typically with a 2D DP table `dp[i][j]` representing whether the first `i` characters of `s` form a subsequence of the first `j` characters of `t`. This has a higher time and space complexity (O(m*n)) compared to the two-pointer approach (O(n)).

Repeatedly using `indexOf` to find characters of `s` in `t` can work but is generally less efficient than the two-pointer method, especially if `indexOf` itself is not optimized for this specific sequential search pattern.

valid-sudoku.intuition: The core idea is to efficiently check for duplicates within each row, column, and 3x3 subgrid. Using hash sets allows for O(1) average time complexity for checking if a number has already been seen in a particular region.
valid-sudoku.pattern_connection: This problem directly utilizes the Arrays & Hashing pattern by employing hash sets to track seen elements, enabling quick duplicate detection across different constraints (rows, columns, boxes).

steps.title: Initialize Data Structures
steps.detail: Create three arrays of hash sets: one for rows, one for columns, and one for 3x3 subgrids.

steps.title: Iterate Through Board
steps.detail: Traverse each cell of the 9x9 Sudoku board.

steps.title: Check for Duplicates
steps.detail: For each non-empty cell, check if the number already exists in the corresponding row, column, and subgrid hash sets.

steps.title: Add to Sets
steps.detail: If no duplicate is found, add the number to the respective row, column, and subgrid hash sets.

steps.title: Handle Invalid Input
steps.detail: If a duplicate is found at any point, immediately return false.

steps.title: Return True
steps.detail: If the entire board is traversed without finding any duplicates, return true.

edge_cases: An empty board (though constraints usually prevent this).
A board filled with only '.' characters.
A board with invalid characters other than '.' and '1'-'9'.
A board where a row, column, or box is valid but another is not.
A board that is almost valid but has a single duplicate.
A board that is completely filled and valid.

alternatives.tradeoff: Checking each row, column, and box using nested loops without hash sets would lead to a much higher time complexity, likely O(n^3) or worse, compared to the O(n^2) of the hash set approach.

alternatives.approach: Using 2D Arrays instead of Hash Sets
alternatives.tradeoff: Using boolean 2D arrays (e.g., `boolean[9][10]`) to mark seen numbers is also efficient and might have slightly better constant factors than hash sets, but conceptually it's very similar to the hash set approach.

largest-number.intuition: To form the largest number, we need to arrange the given numbers such that concatenating them in that order results in the lexicographically largest string. This requires a custom comparison logic that considers the combined effect of two numbers when placed next to each other.
largest-number.pattern_connection: This problem leverages the Arrays & Hashing pattern by treating numbers as strings and using a custom comparator within a sorting algorithm to arrange them based on a specific concatenation rule.

steps.title: Convert Numbers to Strings
steps.detail: Convert all integers in the input array to their string representations.

steps.title: Define Custom Comparator
steps.detail: Create a comparator that, for any two strings `a` and `b`, compares `b + a` with `a + b`. The order that results in a larger concatenated string is preferred.

steps.title: Sort the Strings
steps.detail: Sort the array of strings using the custom comparator defined in the previous step.

steps.title: Concatenate Sorted Strings
steps.detail: Join the sorted strings together to form the final result string.

steps.title: Handle Leading Zeros
steps.detail: If the resulting string starts with '0' and has more than one character, it means all numbers were zeros, so return '0'.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: Return the concatenated string, which represents the largest possible number.

edge_cases: Input array contains a mix of zeros and other numbers.
Input array contains numbers with different lengths.

alternatives.tradeoff: Generating all permutations of the numbers and checking each concatenated string would be computationally infeasible due to the factorial time complexity (O(n! * n)), whereas the custom sort approach is O(n log n * k) where k is the average number of digits.

two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.intuition: Since the input array is sorted, we can efficiently find the pair of numbers that sum up to the target by using two pointers. One pointer starts at the beginning and the other at the end, moving inwards based on whether the current sum is too small, too large, or exactly the target.
two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of the Two Pointers pattern, specifically tailored for sorted arrays, allowing for a linear time solution by intelligently narrowing down the search space.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Initialize a left pointer `l` to the start of the array (index 0) and a right pointer `r` to the end of the array (index `n-1`).

steps.title: Iterate While Pointers Don't Cross
steps.detail: Continue the process as long as the left pointer `l` is less than the right pointer `r`.

steps.title: Calculate Current Sum
steps.detail: Calculate the sum of the elements at the current left and right pointers: `currentSum = numbers[l] + numbers[r]`.

steps.title: Compare Sum with Target
steps.detail: If `currentSum` equals the `target`, we have found the pair; return their indices (l+1 and r+1).

steps.title: Adjust Pointers
steps.detail: If `currentSum` is less than `target`, increment the left pointer `l` to increase the sum. If `currentSum` is greater than `target`, decrement the right pointer `r` to decrease the sum.

steps.title: Handle No Solution
steps.detail: If the loop finishes without finding a pair, it implies no solution exists (though the problem guarantees one).

edge_cases: The two numbers that sum to the target are adjacent.
The target requires the smallest and largest numbers in the array.
The target requires two numbers that are identical (if duplicates are allowed and present).
The array contains only two elements.
The target is very large or very small.

alternatives.tradeoff: A hash map approach (similar to the original Two Sum problem) would work but doesn't take advantage of the sorted nature of the array, resulting in O(n) time complexity and O(n) space complexity, whereas the two-pointer approach achieves O(n) time and O(1) space.
Checking every possible pair using nested loops would have a time complexity of O(n^2), which is significantly less efficient than the O(n) two-pointer solution.

evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.intuition: Reverse Polish Notation (RPN) naturally lends itself to a stack-based evaluation. Operands are pushed onto the stack, and when an operator is encountered, the required operands are popped, the operation is performed, and the result is pushed back.
evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.pattern_connection: This problem is a direct application of the Stack pattern. The LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) nature of a stack perfectly models the way operands are processed and results are stored in RPN.

steps.title: Initialize Stack
steps.detail: Create an empty stack to store operands (integers).

steps.title: Iterate Through Tokens
steps.detail: Process each token in the input array one by one.

steps.title: Handle Operands
steps.detail: If the token is a number, convert it to an integer and push it onto the stack.

steps.title: Handle Operators
steps.detail: If the token is an operator ('+', '-', '*', '/'), pop the top two operands from the stack.

steps.title: Perform Operation and Push Result
steps.detail: Perform the operation using the popped operands (paying attention to order for subtraction and division) and push the result back onto the stack.

steps.title: Return Final Result
steps.detail: After processing all tokens, the final result will be the only element left on the stack; pop and return it.

edge_cases: Invalid tokens in the input array.
Not enough operands for an operator.
Too many operands left on the stack at the end (malformed expression).
Input array contains only a single number.
Handling negative numbers as operands or results.

alternatives.tradeoff: A recursive approach could be devised, but it would likely be more complex to implement and manage the state compared to the straightforward iterative stack-based solution. It might also lead to stack overflow issues for deep expressions.

alternatives.approach: Two Stacks (one for operands, one for operators)
alternatives.tradeoff: While possible, using two stacks is unnecessarily complex for RPN evaluation. A single stack suffices because the structure of RPN dictates when operands are available for operations.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.intuition: The array is sorted but rotated, meaning it consists of two sorted subarrays. We can use a modified binary search to efficiently find the minimum element by comparing the middle element with the rightmost element to determine which half contains the pivot (the minimum value).
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.pattern_connection: This problem is a prime example of applying the Binary Search pattern to a variation of a sorted array. The key is adapting the standard binary search conditions to handle the rotation point.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Set left pointer `l` to 0 and right pointer `r` to the last index of the array.

steps.title: Binary Search Loop
steps.detail: Continue the search as long as `l < r`.

steps.title: Calculate Midpoint
steps.detail: Calculate the middle index `mid = l + (r - l) / 2` to avoid potential integer overflow.

steps.title: Compare Mid with Right
steps.detail: Compare `nums[mid]` with `nums[r]`. If `nums[mid] < nums[r]`, the minimum must be in the left half (including `mid`), so set `r = mid`.

steps.title: Adjust Search Space
steps.detail: If `nums[mid] > nums[r]`, the minimum must be in the right half (excluding `mid`), so set `l = mid + 1`. If `nums[mid] == nums[r]`, we can safely discard the rightmost element by setting `r--` to handle duplicates.

steps.title: Return Minimum
steps.detail: When the loop terminates (`l == r`), `nums[l]` (or `nums[r]`) will be the minimum element.

edge_cases: The array is not rotated (already sorted).
The array has only one element.
The minimum element is the first element.
The minimum element is the last element.

alternatives.tradeoff: Iterating through the array linearly and keeping track of the minimum element found so far has a time complexity of O(n), which is less efficient than the O(log n) binary search approach.

subtree-of-another-tree.intuition: To determine if tree `t` is a subtree of tree `s`, we need to check two conditions: either `s` itself is identical to `t`, or `t` is a subtree of `s`'s left child, or `t` is a subtree of `s`'s right child. This suggests a recursive approach.
subtree-of-another-tree.pattern_connection: This problem involves traversing tree structures, making it a natural fit for recursion and tree-based algorithms. The pattern is essentially Tree Traversal combined with Tree Comparison.

steps.title: Base Cases for Main Function
steps.detail: If the main tree `s` is null, return false (unless `t` is also null, which is handled by the helper). If the subtree `t` is null, return true (an empty tree is a subtree of any tree).

steps.title: Check for Identical Trees
steps.detail: Implement a helper function `isSameTree(s, t)` that checks if two trees are structurally identical and have the same node values. Call this function with the current nodes of `s` and `t`.

steps.title: Recursive Calls
steps.detail: If `isSameTree(s, t)` returns true, then `t` is a subtree of `s`, so return true.

steps.title: Recurse on Children
steps.detail: If the trees are not identical at the current node, recursively call the main function `isSubtree(s.left, t)` and `isSubtree(s.right, t)`. Return true if either of these calls returns true.

steps.title: Handle No Match
steps.detail: If none of the above conditions are met after traversing `s`, return false.

steps.title: Helper Function Logic
steps.detail: The `isSameTree` helper function should also use recursion: base cases for null nodes, check current node values, and recursively call for left and right children.

edge_cases: The subtree `t` is identical to the main tree `s`.
The subtree `t` is found in the left child of `s`.
The subtree `t` is found in the right child of `s`.
The subtree `t` does not exist in `s`.

alternatives.tradeoff: Serialize both trees `s` and `t` into strings (e.g., using pre-order traversal with null markers). Then, check if the serialized string of `t` is a substring of the serialized string of `s`. This can be simpler to code but might be less efficient due to string operations and potentially larger memory usage.

design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.intuition: A Trie (prefix tree) is ideal for this problem because it efficiently stores words based on their prefixes. Searching for words, especially with wildcards, can be done by traversing the Trie, exploring different paths when a wildcard is encountered.
design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.pattern_connection: This problem directly utilizes the Trie data structure pattern. The Trie's structure allows for efficient prefix-based searching and insertion, which is crucial for handling word storage and wildcard searches.

steps.title: Define TrieNode Structure
steps.detail: Create a `TrieNode` class containing an array (or map) of children nodes (e.g., size 26 for lowercase English letters) and a boolean flag `isEndOfWord`.

steps.title: Initialize Trie
steps.detail: Create a root `TrieNode` for the main Trie structure.

steps.title: Implement `addWord`
steps.detail: Iterate through the characters of the word to be added. For each character, traverse or create the corresponding child node in the Trie. Mark the `isEndOfWord` flag of the final node as true.

steps.title: Implement `search` (Recursive Helper)
steps.detail: Create a recursive helper function `searchHelper(node, word, index)` that takes the current node, the word, and the current index in the word.

steps.title: Handle Wildcards and Characters
steps.detail: Inside the helper: if the character is '.', recursively call `searchHelper` for all possible children. If the character is a letter, move to the corresponding child node. Check `isEndOfWord` at the end of the word.

steps.title: Implement `search` (Main)
steps.detail: The main `search` function calls the helper starting from the root node and index 0.

edge_cases: Word search with multiple consecutive wildcards.
Word search where a wildcard matches the end of a word.
Adding the same word multiple times.

alternatives.tradeoff: Storing all words in a hash set allows for O(L) average time complexity for exact word search (where L is word length), but it doesn't efficiently handle wildcard searches. Implementing wildcard search with a hash set would likely require iterating through all stored words, leading to a much worse time complexity (potentially O(N*L) where N is the number of words).

house-robber.intuition: The decision to rob a house depends on whether the previous house was robbed. If we rob the current house, we cannot rob the previous one. If we don't rob the current house, we can take the maximum amount robbed from the previous house. This overlapping subproblem structure suggests dynamic programming.
house-robber.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic example of the 1-D Dynamic Programming pattern. The optimal solution for robbing `i` houses can be derived from the optimal solutions for robbing `i-1` and `i-2` houses.

steps.title: Define DP State
steps.detail: Let `dp[i]` be the maximum amount of money that can be robbed from the first `i` houses.

steps.title: Base Cases
steps.detail: Initialize `dp[0] = 0` (no houses, no money). If there's at least one house, `dp[1] = nums[0]` (rob the first house).

steps.title: Recurrence Relation
steps.detail:
For `i` from 2 to `n` (where `n` is the number of houses), the maximum amount is the greater of: 
1. Robbing house `i`: `nums[i-1] + dp[i-2]` (cannot rob house `i-1`).
2. Not robbing house `i`: `dp[i-1]`.

steps.title: Iteration
steps.detail: Iterate through the houses from the third house onwards, applying the recurrence relation to fill the `dp` array.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.detail: The maximum amount that can be robbed from all `n` houses is `dp[n]`.

steps.title: Space Optimization (Optional)
steps.detail: Notice that `dp[i]` only depends on `dp[i-1]` and `dp[i-2]`. We can optimize space by using only two variables to store the previous two maximum amounts instead of a full DP array.

edge_cases: Input array with only one house.
Large house values that might cause overflow if not handled carefully (though standard integer types are usually sufficient).
The optimal strategy involves skipping multiple houses.

alternatives.tradeoff: A top-down recursive approach with memoization can also solve this problem. It follows the same recurrence relation but uses a memoization table (similar to the DP array) to store results of subproblems. This has the same time complexity O(n) but might incur slightly more overhead due to recursive function calls and potentially use more space if the recursion depth is large.

A simple greedy approach (e.g., always robbing the house with the most money) will not work because it doesn't consider the constraint that adjacent houses cannot be robbed. This can lead to suboptimal solutions.

house-robber-ii.intuition: Since the houses are in a circle, the first and last houses cannot be robbed together. This means we can solve the problem by considering two independent linear house robber problems: one excluding the last house and one excluding the first house. The maximum of these two results will be the answer.
house-robber-ii.pattern_connection: This problem is a variation of the 1D DP pattern, specifically the House Robber problem. The circular arrangement requires a modification to the standard linear DP by breaking it into two linear subproblems.

steps.title: Handle Base Cases
steps.detail: If there's only one house, return its value; if there are two, return the maximum of the two.

steps.title: Define DP State
steps.detail: Let dp[i] be the maximum amount of money that can be robbed from houses 0 to i.

steps.title: Formulate Recurrence Relation
steps.detail: The recurrence is dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]), representing the choice to either skip house i or rob house i.

steps.title: Solve for First Subproblem
steps.detail: Calculate the maximum amount robbing houses from index 0 to n-2 (excluding the last house).

steps.title: Solve for Second Subproblem
steps.detail: Calculate the maximum amount robbing houses from index 1 to n-1 (excluding the first house).

steps.title: Return Maximum
steps.detail: The final answer is the maximum of the results from the two subproblems.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach involves exploring all possible combinations of robbing houses, leading to exponential time complexity and is highly inefficient.

single-number.intuition: The XOR operation has the property that a number XORed with itself results in 0, and a number XORed with 0 results in the number itself. By XORing all elements in the array, pairs of identical numbers will cancel each other out (become 0), leaving only the single, unique number.
single-number.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of the Bit Manipulation pattern, specifically leveraging the properties of the XOR operator.

steps.title: Initialize Result
steps.detail: Initialize a variable, say 'result', to 0.

steps.title: Iterate Through Array
steps.detail: Loop through each number in the input array.

steps.title: Apply XOR Operation
steps.detail: In each iteration, XOR the current number with the 'result' variable and update 'result'.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: After iterating through all numbers, the value of 'result' will be the single number that appears only once.

edge_cases: Array where the single number is at the beginning or end

alternatives.tradeoff: Using a hash set involves adding elements and removing them if they appear twice, which has O(n) time complexity but requires O(n) space complexity, unlike the O(1) space complexity of the XOR approach.
Sorting the array and then iterating to find the single element works but has a time complexity of O(n log n), which is less efficient than the O(n) time complexity of the XOR approach.

reverse-integer.intuition: To reverse an integer, we can extract digits from the original number one by one using the modulo operator and build the reversed number by multiplying the current reversed number by 10 and adding the extracted digit. We must also handle potential integer overflow.
reverse-integer.pattern_connection: This problem falls under the Bit Manipulation pattern, although it primarily uses arithmetic operations. The core idea involves digit manipulation and careful handling of integer limits, similar to problems that might involve bitwise checks for overflow.

steps.title: Initialize Reversed Integer
steps.detail: Initialize a variable 'reversedNum' to 0.

steps.title: Iterate While Number is Not Zero
steps.detail: Loop as long as the input number 'x' is not zero.

steps.title: Extract Last Digit
steps.detail: Get the last digit using the modulo operator: 'digit = x % 10'.

steps.title: Check for Overflow
steps.detail: Before updating 'reversedNum', check if multiplying it by 10 and adding 'digit' would cause an overflow (exceed Integer.MAX_VALUE or fall below Integer.MIN_VALUE).

steps.title: Build Reversed Integer
steps.detail: Update 'reversedNum': 'reversedNum = reversedNum * 10 + digit'.

steps.title: Remove Last Digit
steps.detail: Remove the last digit from 'x': 'x = x / 10'.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: If overflow occurred, return 0; otherwise, return 'reversedNum'.

edge_cases: Input is a single digit number

alternatives.tradeoff: Converting the integer to a string, reversing the string, and converting it back to an integer is simpler to implement but less efficient due to the overhead of string operations and potential issues with handling the sign and overflow.

remove-element.intuition: We can solve this efficiently by using a two-pointer approach. One pointer iterates through the array, and another pointer keeps track of the position where the next non-removed element should be placed. This allows us to overwrite elements to be removed with valid elements in a single pass.
remove-element.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic example of the Arrays & Hashing pattern, specifically utilizing the Two Pointers technique for in-place array modification.

steps.title: Initialize Write Pointer
steps.detail: Initialize a 'write_index' (or 'k') to 0, which will track the position for the next element to keep.

steps.title: Iterate Through Array
steps.detail: Use a 'read_index' (or 'i') to iterate through the entire input array 'nums' from beginning to end.

steps.title: Check Element Value
steps.detail: For each element 'nums[read_index]', check if it is equal to the value 'val' that needs to be removed.

steps.title: Keep Element
steps.detail: If 'nums[read_index]' is NOT equal to 'val', copy it to the position indicated by 'write_index': 'nums[write_index] = nums[read_index]'.

steps.title: Increment Write Pointer
steps.detail: If an element was kept (i.e., not equal to 'val'), increment 'write_index' by 1.

steps.title: Return New Length
steps.detail: After the loop finishes, 'write_index' will represent the number of elements remaining in the array (the new effective length). Return 'write_index'.

edge_cases: All elements need to be removed
No elements need to be removed
The element to remove is at the beginning/end
The array contains duplicates of the element to remove

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach involves creating a new array and copying only the desired elements, which is simpler conceptually but requires O(n) extra space and is not an in-place solution.
For each element to remove, swap it with the last element and decrease the effective array size. This is efficient (O(n) time, O(1) space) but changes the relative order of the remaining elements.

unique-email-addresses.intuition: To count unique email addresses, we need to normalize each email according to specific rules: ignore dots in the local part and treat everything after a '+' in the local part as ignored. After normalization, we can use a set to efficiently store and count the unique addresses.
unique-email-addresses.pattern_connection: This problem utilizes the Arrays & Hashing pattern, specifically employing a Set data structure for efficient uniqueness checks after applying string manipulation rules.

steps.title: Initialize a Set
steps.detail: Create an empty HashSet to store the normalized email addresses.

steps.title: Iterate Through Emails
steps.detail: Loop through each email address in the input array.

steps.title: Split into Local and Domain
steps.detail: Split the current email address at the '@' symbol into its local and domain parts.

steps.title: Normalize Local Part
steps.detail: Process the local part: remove all '.' characters and ignore everything after the first '+' character.

steps.title: Reconstruct Normalized Email
steps.detail: Combine the normalized local part with the original domain part, separated by '@'.

steps.title: Add to Set
steps.detail: Add the fully normalized email address to the HashSet.

steps.title: Return Set Size
steps.detail: The final answer is the size of the HashSet, which represents the count of unique normalized email addresses.

edge_cases: Email with multiple '+' characters in local part
Email with dots and '+' characters combined
Email with only a local part (invalid format, but consider)
Email with only a domain part (invalid format, but consider)
Emails with unusual characters (if allowed by constraints)

alternatives.tradeoff: A HashMap could be used to store normalized emails as keys and a count as values, but a HashSet is more direct for simply counting unique items, offering similar time complexity but potentially simpler logic.

product-of-array-except-self.intuition: We can calculate the product of all elements except self by computing prefix products (product of elements to the left) and suffix products (product of elements to the right) separately. The product except self at index `i` is then the product of the prefix product at `i-1` and the suffix product at `i+1`.
product-of-array-except-self.pattern_connection: This problem is a variation of the Arrays & Hashing pattern, often solved using prefix/suffix computations. It cleverly avoids division by breaking down the problem into left and right product calculations.

steps.title: Initialize Result Array
steps.detail: Create an output array 'answer' of the same size as the input array 'nums'.

steps.title: Calculate Prefix Products
steps.detail: Iterate from left to right. For each index `i`, `answer[i]` will store the product of all elements to the left of `i`. Initialize `answer[0]` to 1.

steps.title: Calculate Suffix Products
steps.detail: Iterate from right to left. Maintain a variable `suffixProduct` (initially 1). For each index `i`, multiply `answer[i]` (which currently holds the prefix product) by `suffixProduct`, then update `suffixProduct` by multiplying it with `nums[i]`.

steps.title: Combine Prefix and Suffix
steps.detail: During the right-to-left pass, `answer[i]` is updated to `answer[i] * suffixProduct`, effectively becoming the product of elements to its left multiplied by the product of elements to its right.

steps.title: Return Result Array
steps.detail: The 'answer' array now contains the product of all elements except self for each index.

edge_cases: Array contains zeros (one zero, multiple zeros)
Large numbers that might cause overflow if intermediate products are not handled carefully (though the problem usually guarantees results fit in int)

alternatives.tradeoff: Calculate the total product of all elements and then divide by the element at each index. This is simpler but disallowed by the problem constraints and fails if the array contains zeros.
For each element, iterate through the rest of the array to calculate the product. This has a time complexity of O(n^2), which is inefficient compared to the O(n) prefix/suffix product approach.

reverse-string.intuition: To reverse a string in-place, we can use two pointers: one starting at the beginning of the string and the other at the end. We swap the characters pointed to by these pointers and move them towards the center until they meet or cross.
reverse-string.pattern_connection: This problem is a straightforward application of the Two Pointers pattern, commonly used for in-place modifications of sequences like arrays or strings.

steps.title: Convert to Character Array
steps.detail: Convert the input string into a character array for mutable access.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Initialize a left pointer `left` to 0 and a right pointer `right` to the last index of the array (length - 1).

steps.title: Loop While Pointers Don't Cross
steps.detail: Continue the process as long as `left` is less than `right`.

steps.title: Swap Characters
steps.detail: Swap the characters at the `left` and `right` indices.

steps.title: Move Pointers
steps.detail: Increment `left` and decrement `right` to move the pointers towards the center.

steps.title: Convert Back to String
steps.detail: Convert the modified character array back into a string.

edge_cases: String with even number of characters
String with odd number of characters
String with special characters or numbers

alternatives.tradeoff: Create a new StringBuilder, append characters from the original string in reverse order, and convert back. This is simpler but uses extra space (O(n)) and is not an in-place solution.
A recursive approach can swap characters similarly, but it typically uses more stack space (O(n)) compared to the iterative two-pointer approach's O(1) space complexity.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.intuition: We can use a sliding window approach. The window is valid if the number of characters that need to be replaced (window size minus the count of the most frequent character) is less than or equal to the allowed replacements `k`. We expand the window to the right and shrink it from the left when the condition is violated.
longest-repeating-character-replacement.pattern_connection: This problem is a prime example of the Sliding Window pattern, where we maintain a dynamic window to find the longest substring satisfying a given condition.

steps.title: Initialize Variables
steps.detail: Initialize `left = 0`, `maxLength = 0`, `maxFrequency = 0`, and a frequency map (e.g., an array of size 26 for uppercase English letters) to store character counts within the window.

steps.title: Expand Window
steps.detail: Iterate through the string with a `right` pointer from 0 to `n-1`.

steps.title: Update Frequency Map
steps.detail: Increment the count of the character `s[right]` in the frequency map and update `maxFrequency` if the current character's count exceeds it.

steps.title: Check Window Validity
steps.detail: Calculate the number of characters to replace: `replacementsNeeded = (right - left + 1) - maxFrequency`. If `replacementsNeeded > k`, the window is invalid.

steps.title: Shrink Window
steps.detail: If the window is invalid (`replacementsNeeded > k`), decrement the count of `s[left]` in the frequency map and increment `left` to shrink the window from the left.

steps.title: Update Max Length
steps.detail: After potentially shrinking the window, update `maxLength = max(maxLength, right - left + 1)`.

steps.title: Return Max Length
steps.detail: After the loop finishes, `maxLength` holds the length of the longest valid substring.

edge_cases: k is very large (larger than string length)
String contains only one distinct character
The longest substring occurs at the beginning or end of the string

alternatives.tradeoff: Check every possible substring and for each substring, count the characters needed for replacement. This has a time complexity of O(n^3) or O(n^2) with optimization, which is significantly less efficient than the O(n) sliding window approach.

generate-parentheses.intuition: The problem requires generating all valid combinations of parentheses. This can be achieved by building the combinations recursively, ensuring at each step that we don't add more closing parentheses than opening ones, and that we don't exceed the total allowed number of each.
generate-parentheses.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic example of backtracking, where we explore all possible paths (adding '(' or ')') and prune invalid ones. A stack-like behavior is implicitly used in the recursion to manage the state of open parentheses.

steps.title: Initialize Result and Backtracking Function
steps.detail: Create a list to store the valid combinations and define a recursive helper function that takes the current string, the count of open parentheses, and the count of closed parentheses.

steps.title: Base Case for Recursion
steps.detail: If the length of the current string equals 2 * n (where n is the number of pairs), add the string to the result list and return.

steps.title: Add Opening Parenthesis
steps.detail: If the count of open parentheses is less than n, recursively call the function with an added '(' and increment the open count.

steps.title: Add Closing Parenthesis
steps.detail: If the count of closed parentheses is less than the count of open parentheses, recursively call the function with an added ')' and increment the closed count.

steps.title: Backtrack
steps.detail: The recursive calls naturally handle backtracking by returning to the previous state after exploring a path.

alternatives.tradeoff: Can be less intuitive to implement compared to the recursive backtracking approach.

daily-temperatures.intuition: For each day, we need to find the next warmer day. This implies looking forward in the temperature list. A stack can efficiently keep track of days for which we haven't found a warmer day yet, allowing us to quickly find the next warmer day when a higher temperature is encountered.
daily-temperatures.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of a monotonic stack (specifically, a decreasing monotonic stack). The stack stores indices of days, and we pop elements when we find a warmer day, thus maintaining the order and enabling efficient lookups.

steps.title: Initialize Result Array and Stack
steps.detail: Create an integer array `answer` of the same size as `temperatures`, initialized with zeros, and an empty stack to store indices.

steps.title: Iterate Through Temperatures
steps.detail: Loop through the `temperatures` array from left to right using an index `i`.

steps.title: Process Stack
steps.detail: While the stack is not empty and the temperature at the current index `i` is greater than the temperature at the index at the top of the stack, pop the index `prevIndex` from the stack.

steps.title: Calculate Difference
steps.detail: For the popped `prevIndex`, set `answer[prevIndex]` to `i - prevIndex`, which is the number of days until the next warmer temperature.

steps.title: Push Current Index
steps.detail: Push the current index `i` onto the stack.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: After iterating through all temperatures, return the `answer` array.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach has a time complexity of O(n^2) because it involves nested loops to compare each day with all subsequent days, which is inefficient for larger inputs.

car-fleet.intuition: Cars that arrive at the destination at the same time or a car that arrives earlier than the one in front of it will form a fleet. By calculating the arrival time of each car and processing them from closest to the destination backwards, we can use a stack to keep track of the fleets.
car-fleet.pattern_connection: This problem can be solved using a stack to manage the fleets. By processing cars from closest to the destination to furthest, we can determine if a car merges with the fleet ahead of it, effectively using the stack to count the number of distinct fleets.

steps.title: Combine Position and Speed, Sort
steps.detail: Create pairs of (position, speed) for each car. Sort these pairs based on the car's starting position in ascending order.

steps.title: Calculate Arrival Times
steps.detail: For each car, calculate its arrival time at the destination using the formula: `arrivalTime = (target - position) / speed`.

steps.title: Initialize Stack and Fleet Count
steps.detail: Initialize an empty stack to store arrival times of fleet leaders and a fleet count variable to 0.

steps.title: Iterate Through Cars (Backwards)
steps.detail: Iterate through the sorted cars from the one closest to the destination to the one furthest away.

steps.title: Fleet Formation Logic
steps.detail: If the stack is empty or the current car's arrival time is greater than the arrival time at the top of the stack, it means this car forms a new fleet. Push its arrival time onto the stack and increment the fleet count.

steps.title: Return Fleet Count
steps.detail: The final fleet count is the number of elements remaining in the stack after processing all cars.

edge_cases: All cars arrive at the same time
All cars form a single fleet
Each car forms its own fleet

alternatives.tradeoff: Simulating each car's movement step-by-step and checking for fleet formation would be computationally expensive and likely exceed time limits due to its high time complexity.

search-insert-position.intuition: Given a sorted array, we need to find the index of a target value. If the target is not present, we need to find the index where it would be inserted to maintain the sorted order. Binary search is the most efficient algorithm for searching in a sorted array.
search-insert-position.pattern_connection: This problem directly applies the Binary Search pattern. The sorted nature of the input array is the key indicator that binary search should be used for efficient searching and finding the insertion point.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Set `low` to 0 and `high` to the last index of the array (`nums.length - 1`).

steps.title: Binary Search Loop
steps.detail: While `low` is less than or equal to `high`, calculate the middle index `mid`.

steps.title: Target Found
steps.detail: If `nums[mid]` equals the `target`, return `mid`.

steps.title: Adjust Search Range (Target Greater)
steps.detail: If `nums[mid]` is less than the `target`, the target must be in the right half, so update `low` to `mid + 1`.

steps.title: Adjust Search Range (Target Smaller)
steps.detail: If `nums[mid]` is greater than the `target`, the target must be in the left half, so update `high` to `mid - 1`.

steps.title: Target Not Found (Insertion Point)
steps.detail: If the loop finishes without finding the target, `low` will be the correct insertion index. Return `low`.

edge_cases: Target is smaller than all elements
Target is larger than all elements
Target is present in the array
Target is not present but falls within the range

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach has a time complexity of O(n), which is less efficient than binary search's O(log n) for large arrays.

time-based-key-value-store.intuition: We need to store key-value pairs with timestamps and retrieve the value for a given key at a specific timestamp. Since timestamps for a given key are added in increasing order, we can use binary search on the timestamps associated with each key to efficiently find the correct value.
time-based-key-value-store.pattern_connection: This problem utilizes Binary Search within a hash map structure. The values associated with each key are stored in a way that allows for binary search (e.g., a list of pairs sorted by timestamp), enabling efficient retrieval based on time.

steps.title: Data Structure Design
steps.detail: Use a HashMap where keys are the string keys and values are lists of pairs (timestamp, value). Each list should be kept sorted by timestamp.

steps.title: Implement `set` Operation
steps.detail: When `set(key, value, timestamp)` is called, retrieve the list associated with the `key`. If the list doesn't exist, create it. Add the `(timestamp, value)` pair to the list. Ensure the list remains sorted by timestamp (or add it in a way that maintains order).

steps.title: Implement `get` Operation
steps.detail: When `get(key, timestamp)` is called, retrieve the list of `(ts, val)` pairs for the given `key`.

steps.title: Binary Search for Timestamp
steps.detail: Perform a binary search on the list of pairs to find the largest timestamp `ts` that is less than or equal to the given `timestamp`.

steps.title: Return Value
steps.detail: If such a timestamp is found, return its corresponding value. Otherwise, return an empty string.

edge_cases: Timestamp is earlier than any stored timestamp for the key
Timestamp exactly matches a stored timestamp
Multiple values stored for the same key at different timestamps
Timestamp is very large or very small

alternatives.tradeoff: Iterating through all stored timestamps for a key would result in O(N) time complexity for the `get` operation, where N is the number of entries for that key, which is less efficient than the O(log N) achieved with binary search.

last-stone-weight.intuition: The process involves repeatedly selecting the two heaviest stones, smashing them, and potentially putting a new stone back. A max-heap (priority queue) is the ideal data structure to efficiently retrieve the two largest elements at each step.
last-stone-weight.pattern_connection: This problem is a direct application of the Heap (Max Heap) data structure. The heap allows for efficient retrieval of the maximum elements (O(log n)) and insertion of new elements (O(log n)), which is crucial for simulating the stone-smashing process.

steps.title: Initialize Max Heap
steps.detail: Create a max heap (PriorityQueue in Java with reverse order comparator) and add all the stone weights to it.

steps.title: Process Stones
steps.detail: While the heap contains more than one stone:

steps.title: Extract Heaviest Stones
steps.detail: Remove the two largest elements (stones) from the heap, let's call them `stone1` and `stone2` (where `stone1 >= stone2`).

steps.title: Calculate Difference and Add Back
steps.detail: If `stone1` is not equal to `stone2`, calculate their difference (`stone1 - stone2`) and add this new stone weight back into the heap.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: After the loop finishes (heap has 0 or 1 element), if the heap is not empty, return the single remaining element. Otherwise, return 0.

edge_cases: All stones have the same weight
Stones result in a final weight of 0
Stones result in a final positive weight

alternatives.tradeoff: Sorting the array after each smash operation would lead to a time complexity of O(n^2 log n), which is significantly slower than the heap-based O(n log n) solution.

decode-ways.intuition: To find the number of ways to decode a message, we can think about the last digit(s). A digit can be decoded on its own (if not '0'), or it can be decoded with the previous digit if they form a valid two-digit number (10-26). This suggests a dynamic programming approach where `dp[i]` represents the number of ways to decode the string up to index `i`.
decode-ways.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic example of Dynamic Programming (1-D DP). The number of ways to decode a string up to a certain point depends on the number of ways to decode it up to previous points, exhibiting optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems.

steps.title: Initialize DP Array
steps.detail: Create a DP array `dp` of size `n+1`, where `n` is the length of the string. `dp[i]` will store the number of ways to decode the substring `s[0...i-1]`.

steps.title: Base Cases
steps.detail: Set `dp[0] = 1` (representing one way to decode an empty string). If the first character `s[0]` is not '0', set `dp[1] = 1`.

steps.title: Iterate and Calculate DP Values
steps.detail: Iterate from `i = 2` to `n`.

steps.title: One-Digit Decoding
steps.detail: Consider the `i`-th character (which corresponds to `s[i-1]`). If it's not '0', it can be decoded independently. Add `dp[i-1]` to `dp[i]`.

steps.title: Two-Digit Decoding
steps.detail: Consider the `i`-th and `(i-1)`-th characters (which correspond to `s[i-2]` and `s[i-1]`). Form the two-digit number. If it's between 10 and 26 (inclusive), it can be decoded as a pair. Add `dp[i-2]` to `dp[i]`.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: The final answer is `dp[n]`.

edge_cases: String contains '0' not preceded by '1' or '2'
String contains consecutive invalid two-digit combinations (e.g., '30')
String with all valid single-digit decodings

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach is conceptually similar to DP but might lead to stack overflow issues for very long strings if not implemented carefully. DP is often preferred for its iterative nature.
This leads to exponential time complexity due to recomputing the same subproblems multiple times, making it highly inefficient.

jump-game-ii.intuition: We want to reach the end of the array in the minimum number of jumps. At each step, we should greedily choose the jump that allows us to reach the farthest possible position. This ensures we cover the maximum ground with each jump, minimizing the total jumps needed.
jump-game-ii.pattern_connection: This problem is solved using a Greedy approach. At each position, we make a locally optimal choice (jumping to the position that maximizes our reach) hoping it leads to a globally optimal solution (minimum jumps).

steps.title: Initialize Variables
steps.detail: Initialize `jumps = 0`, `currentReach = 0`, and `maxReach = 0`.

steps.title: Iterate Through Array
steps.detail: Iterate through the array from index `i = 0` up to `n-2` (where `n` is the array length). We stop at `n-2` because if we reach this index, we can always jump to the last index in one more step.

steps.title: Update Maximum Reach
steps.detail: For each index `i`, update `maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i])`. This tracks the farthest index reachable from any position within the current jump's range.

steps.title: Check if Jump is Needed
steps.detail: If the current index `i` equals `currentReach`, it means we have reached the end of the range covered by the previous jump. We must make a new jump.

steps.title: Perform Jump
steps.detail: Increment `jumps` by 1. Update `currentReach` to `maxReach`. This sets the boundary for the next jump.

steps.title: Return Jumps
steps.detail: After the loop, return the total number of `jumps`.

edge_cases: It's always possible to reach the last index
Array where multiple jumps are needed
Array where only one jump is needed from the start

alternatives.tradeoff: A DP approach (e.g., `dp[i] = min jumps to reach index i`) would have a time complexity of O(n^2), which is less efficient than the greedy approach's O(n).
BFS can also solve this problem in O(n) time by treating jumps as levels, but the greedy approach is often considered slightly more straightforward to implement for this specific problem.

non-overlapping-intervals.intuition: To maximize the number of non-overlapping intervals, we should greedily select intervals that finish earliest. By sorting intervals by their end times, we ensure that by picking an interval, we leave the maximum room for subsequent intervals.
non-overlapping-intervals.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic example of an interval scheduling problem, which is typically solved using a greedy approach after sorting the intervals based on their end points.

steps.title: Sort Intervals
steps.detail: Sort the given intervals based on their end points in ascending order.

steps.title: Initialize Variables
steps.detail: Initialize a counter for non-overlapping intervals to 1 and store the end time of the first interval.

steps.title: Iterate Through Intervals
steps.detail: Iterate through the sorted intervals starting from the second interval.

steps.title: Check for Overlap
steps.detail: If the current interval's start time is greater than or equal to the end time of the previously selected interval, it does not overlap.

steps.title: Select Non-Overlapping Interval
steps.detail: If there is no overlap, increment the counter and update the end time to the current interval's end time.

steps.title: Return Count
steps.detail: The final count represents the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals.

edge_cases: Intervals with identical start and end points.

alternatives.tradeoff: Sorting by start time and greedily picking the shortest interval can lead to suboptimal solutions, as a short interval might block many other potential intervals.
This approach would involve checking all possible subsets of intervals, leading to exponential time complexity, which is too slow for typical constraints.

happy-number.intuition: A number is happy if repeatedly replacing it with the sum of the squares of its digits eventually leads to 1. If this process enters a cycle that does not include 1, the number is not happy. Detecting cycles is key.
happy-number.pattern_connection: This problem falls under mathematical number theory and cycle detection. The core idea is to simulate the process and use a set to detect if a number repeats, indicating a cycle.

steps.title: Initialize a Set
steps.detail: Create a HashSet to store numbers encountered during the process to detect cycles.

steps.title: Iterative Calculation
steps.detail: Start a loop that continues as long as the current number is not 1 and has not been seen before.

steps.title: Add to Set
steps.detail: Add the current number to the HashSet before calculating the next number.

steps.title: Calculate Next Number
steps.detail: Implement a helper function to calculate the sum of the squares of the digits of the current number.

steps.title: Check for Cycle
steps.detail: If the current number is already in the HashSet, a cycle is detected, and the number is not happy.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: If the loop terminates because the number becomes 1, return true; otherwise (cycle detected), return false.

edge_cases: Input is a number that immediately enters a cycle (e.g., 4).

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach avoids using extra space (HashSet) by using two pointers moving at different speeds, but the logic might be slightly less intuitive than using a set.

plus-one.intuition: Adding one to a number represented as an array of digits is similar to manual addition. We start from the least significant digit and handle carries propagating to the left.
plus-one.pattern_connection: This problem is a direct application of elementary arithmetic, specifically handling carries in addition, within the context of array manipulation.

steps.title: Iterate from Right to Left
steps.detail: Begin iterating through the digits array from the last element (least significant digit) to the first.

steps.title: Increment Digit
steps.detail: Increment the current digit by one.

steps.title: Handle Carry
steps.detail: If the digit becomes 10, set it to 0 and continue to the next digit to the left (carry-over).

steps.title: No Carry
steps.detail: If the digit is less than 10 after incrementing, there is no carry, and the process can stop; return the modified array.

steps.title: Handle Leading Carry
steps.detail: If the loop finishes and a carry remains (meaning the most significant digit became 10), create a new array with an additional element at the beginning set to 1.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: Return the modified digits array or the new array if a leading carry occurred.

alternatives.approach: Convert to Integer, Add, Convert Back
alternatives.tradeoff: This approach is simpler conceptually but fails for very large numbers that exceed the maximum integer limits (e.g., `long` in Java).

missing-number.intuition: The XOR operation has the property that `a ^ a = 0` and `a ^ 0 = a`. By XORing all numbers from 0 to n and all numbers in the given array, all numbers present in both will cancel out, leaving only the missing number.
missing-number.pattern_connection: This problem leverages the properties of the XOR bitwise operator, specifically its commutative and associative properties, and the fact that XORing a number with itself results in zero.

steps.title: Determine Range
steps.detail: Identify the expected range of numbers, which is from 0 to n, where n is the length of the input array `nums`.

steps.title: Initialize XOR Result
steps.detail: Initialize a variable, say `missing`, to n. This accounts for the number n which is part of the expected range but might not be in the array indices.

steps.title: XOR with Indices and Values
steps.detail: Iterate through the array from index 0 to n-1. In each iteration, XOR `missing` with the current index `i` and the element `nums[i]`.

steps.title: XOR Properties
steps.detail: The XOR operation `missing ^= i ^ nums[i]` effectively XORs all numbers from 0 to n with all numbers present in the array.

steps.title: Isolate Missing Number
steps.detail: After the loop, `missing` will hold the value of the number that was present in the range 0 to n but absent in the `nums` array, as all other numbers will have been XORed twice (once from the range, once from the array) and cancelled out.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: Return the final value of `missing`.

edge_cases: The array contains all numbers from 0 to n-1.
The array contains duplicates (though problem constraints usually prevent this).

alternatives.tradeoff: Calculate the expected sum (n*(n+1)/2) and subtract the actual sum of the array elements. This can lead to integer overflow for large n.
Add all numbers from the array to a HashSet, then iterate from 0 to n and check which number is not in the set. This uses O(n) extra space.

sum-of-two-integers.intuition: We can simulate binary addition using bitwise operations. The sum without carry can be obtained using XOR, and the carry can be obtained using AND followed by a left shift. We repeat this process until there is no carry.
sum-of-two-integers.pattern_connection: This problem is a direct application of bit manipulation, specifically using XOR (`^`) for addition without carry and AND (`&`) with left shift (`<< 1`) for handling the carry.

steps.title: Initialize Carry
steps.detail: Use a variable, say `carry`, to store the carry bits. Initially, it's calculated from `a` and `b`.

steps.title: Calculate Sum Without Carry
steps.detail: Calculate the sum of `a` and `b` without considering the carry using the XOR operator: `a = a ^ b`.

steps.title: Calculate Carry
steps.detail: Calculate the carry bits by ANDing `a` and `b`, then left-shifting the result by one position: `carry = (a & b) << 1`.

steps.title: Update Operands
steps.detail: Update `b` to be the `carry` calculated in the previous step.

steps.title: Repeat Until No Carry
steps.detail: Continue the process (steps 2-4) as long as `b` (the carry) is not zero.

steps.title: Return Sum
steps.detail: Once `b` becomes zero, `a` holds the final sum, so return `a`.

edge_cases: Adding a positive and a negative number.
Integer overflow scenarios (handled by Java's bitwise operations on `int`).

alternatives.tradeoff: This is the most straightforward approach but is explicitly disallowed by the problem statement.

isomorphic-strings.intuition: Two strings are isomorphic if there's a consistent mapping between characters of the first string to the second. This means each character in `s` must map to exactly one character in `t`, and no two characters in `s` can map to the same character in `t`.
isomorphic-strings.pattern_connection: This problem involves mapping characters between two strings, which is efficiently handled using hash maps (or arrays acting as maps) to store and check character correspondences.

steps.title: Check Lengths
steps.detail: If the lengths of the two strings `s` and `t` are different, they cannot be isomorphic, so return `false`.

steps.title: Initialize Mappings
steps.detail: Create two hash maps: `mapS` to store mappings from `s` to `t`, and `mapT` to store mappings from `t` to `s`.

steps.title: Iterate Through Strings
steps.detail: Loop through the strings character by character from index 0 to length-1.

steps.title: Check Forward Mapping
steps.detail: For the current characters `sChar` and `tChar`: if `sChar` is already in `mapS`, check if its mapped value equals `tChar`. If not, return `false`.

steps.title: Check Backward Mapping
steps.detail: Similarly, if `tChar` is already in `mapT`, check if its mapped value equals `sChar`. If not, return `false`.

steps.title: Establish Mapping
steps.detail: If the mappings are consistent or new, add/update the mappings in both `mapS` (sChar -> tChar) and `mapT` (tChar -> sChar).

steps.title: Return True
steps.detail: If the loop completes without returning `false`, the strings are isomorphic, so return `true`.

edge_cases: Strings where a character maps to multiple characters (e.g., 'ab', 'aa').
Strings where multiple characters map to the same character (e.g., 'aba', 'baa').

alternatives.tradeoff: If the character set is limited (e.g., ASCII), arrays of size 256 can be used instead of HashMaps for potentially faster lookups, but HashMaps are more general.

Transform both strings into a canonical pattern (e.g., replace first occurrence of char 'a' with '0', second with '1', etc.) and check if the patterns are equal. This is conceptually similar but might require careful implementation.

can-place-flowers.intuition: To maximize the number of flowers planted, we should iterate through the flowerbed and plant a flower whenever possible. A flower can be planted in an empty plot if both its adjacent plots are also empty.
can-place-flowers.pattern_connection: This problem involves iterating through an array and checking local conditions (adjacent elements), which is a common pattern solvable with a single pass and careful handling of boundary conditions.

steps.title: Iterate Through Flowerbed
steps.detail: Traverse the `flowerbed` array from the beginning to the end.

steps.title: Check Current Plot
steps.detail: For each plot, check if it is currently empty (value is 0).

steps.title: Check Adjacent Plots
steps.detail: If the current plot is empty, check its left and right neighbors. The left neighbor is empty if it's the start of the array or the plot to the left is 0. The right neighbor is empty if it's the end of the array or the plot to the right is 0.

steps.title: Plant Flower
steps.detail: If the current plot and both its adjacent plots (considering boundaries) are empty, plant a flower by setting the current plot's value to 1 and decrement the `n` count.

steps.title: Check if Done
steps.detail: If `n` becomes 0 at any point, it means we have successfully planted all required flowers, so return `true`.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: After iterating through the entire flowerbed, if `n` is 0, return `true`; otherwise, return `false`.

edge_cases: Flowerbed where all plots are already planted.
Flowerbed where no plots can be planted.
Edge cases: planting at the very beginning or end of the array.

alternatives.tradeoff: Adding a 0 at the beginning and end of the flowerbed array simplifies boundary checks within the loop, making the code slightly cleaner but uses extra space.

find-pivot-index.intuition: A pivot index is where the sum of elements to its left equals the sum of elements to its right. We can efficiently find this by calculating the total sum first, then iterating while maintaining a running left sum and deriving the right sum.
find-pivot-index.pattern_connection: This problem involves prefix sums and array traversal. By pre-calculating the total sum and iteratively updating the left sum, we can determine the right sum in constant time for each index.

steps.title: Calculate Total Sum
steps.detail: Compute the sum of all elements in the `nums` array.

steps.title: Initialize Left Sum
steps.detail: Initialize a variable `leftSum` to 0.

steps.title: Iterate Through Array
steps.detail: Iterate through the `nums` array using an index `i` from 0 to `nums.length - 1`.

steps.title: Calculate Right Sum
steps.detail: For each index `i`, calculate the `rightSum` as `totalSum - leftSum - nums[i]`.

steps.title: Check for Pivot
steps.detail: If `leftSum` is equal to `rightSum`, then `i` is a pivot index. Return `i`.

steps.title: Update Left Sum
steps.detail: Add the current element `nums[i]` to `leftSum` to prepare for the next iteration.

steps.title: Return -1
steps.detail: If the loop completes without finding a pivot index, return -1.

edge_cases: Array with a single element (index 0 is pivot).
Pivot index is the first element (left sum is 0).
Pivot index is the last element (right sum is 0).

alternatives.tradeoff: For each index, calculate the sum of elements to its left and the sum of elements to its right separately. This involves nested loops and has a time complexity of O(n^2), which is less efficient than the prefix sum approach.

range-sum-query-immutable.intuition: Since the array is immutable, we can precompute prefix sums to answer range sum queries efficiently. The sum of a range [i, j] can then be calculated in O(1) time by subtracting the prefix sum up to i-1 from the prefix sum up to j.
range-sum-query-immutable.pattern_connection: This problem directly utilizes the prefix sum technique, a common optimization within the Arrays & Hashing pattern, to enable fast range queries on static data.

steps.title: Initialize Prefix Sum Array
steps.detail: Create a new array, `prefixSum`, of size n+1, where n is the length of the input array `nums`.

steps.title: Compute Prefix Sums
steps.detail: Iterate through `nums` and populate `prefixSum` such that `prefixSum[i+1]` stores the sum of elements from `nums[0]` to `nums[i]`.

steps.title: Implement Sum Range Query
steps.detail: For a query `sumRange(i, j)`, return `prefixSum[j+1] - prefixSum[i]`.

edge_cases: Query range ends at the last index.

alternatives.tradeoff: Calculating the sum for each query by iterating through the range takes O(n) time per query, which is inefficient for multiple queries compared to the O(1) query time of the prefix sum approach.

encode-and-decode-strings.intuition: To encode a list of strings, we need a way to delimit them such that decoding is unambiguous. Prepending the length of each string before the string itself, separated by a delimiter, allows us to reconstruct the original strings.
encode-and-decode-strings.pattern_connection: This problem leverages the Arrays & Hashing pattern by using a custom encoding scheme that treats strings as data to be processed and stored, often involving hash-like structures for mapping or lookups.

steps.title: Encoding Logic
steps.detail: For each string in the list, append its length, a delimiter (e.g., '#'), and the string itself to the encoded result.

steps.title: Decoding Logic Initialization
steps.detail: Initialize an empty list to store the decoded strings and a pointer `i` to the beginning of the encoded string.

steps.title: Extract String Length
steps.detail: Find the position of the delimiter '#' starting from `i` to determine the length of the next string.

steps.title: Extract String
steps.detail: Extract the substring of the calculated length starting after the delimiter and add it to the decoded list.

steps.title: Update Pointer
steps.detail: Advance the pointer `i` past the delimiter and the extracted string to process the next encoded string.

edge_cases: List contains strings with the delimiter character.

alternatives.approach: Using a fixed-size delimiter or escaping characters
alternatives.tradeoff: More complex to implement and potentially less efficient if strings frequently contain the delimiter, whereas the length-prefix method is straightforward and robust.

encode-and-decode-tinyurl.intuition: We need a way to map long URLs to short, unique identifiers and vice-versa. A hash map is ideal for storing these bidirectional mappings, allowing for quick lookups.
encode-and-decode-tinyurl.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of Arrays & Hashing, specifically using hash maps to store and retrieve mappings between original URLs and their shortened versions.

steps.title: Initialize Storage
steps.detail: Create two hash maps: one to map short URLs to long URLs (`shortToLong`) and another to map long URLs to short URLs (`longToShort`). Also, maintain a counter or generator for unique short codes.

steps.title: Encode URL
steps.detail: If the long URL is already in `longToShort`, return its existing short URL. Otherwise, generate a unique short code, store the mapping in both hash maps, and return the short URL.

steps.title: Generate Short Code
steps.detail: Create a unique short identifier, perhaps by hashing the long URL or using a counter and converting it to a base-62 representation.

steps.title: Decode URL
steps.detail: Look up the short URL in the `shortToLong` map and return the corresponding long URL.

edge_cases: Encoding the same URL multiple times.
Ensuring uniqueness of generated short codes under high load.

alternatives.tradeoff: Scalability issues in distributed systems compared to a well-designed hashing or counter-based approach that can be managed across nodes.
Higher probability of collisions, requiring more checks and retries, making it less efficient than deterministic methods for encoding.

subarray-sum-equals-k.intuition: We are looking for subarrays whose sum equals k. If we maintain a running sum, the sum of a subarray from index i to j is `current_sum[j] - current_sum[i-1]`. We want this to equal k, so `current_sum[j] - k = current_sum[i-1]`. This means we can use a hash map to store frequencies of running sums encountered so far.
subarray-sum-equals-k.pattern_connection: This problem is solved efficiently using a hash map to store prefix sums and their frequencies, a common technique within the Arrays & Hashing pattern for problems involving subarray sums.

steps.title: Initialize Variables
steps.detail: Initialize `count = 0`, `currentSum = 0`, and a hash map `prefixSumFreq` to store the frequency of each prefix sum encountered.

steps.title: Add Base Case to Map
steps.detail: Put `(0, 1)` into `prefixSumFreq` to handle subarrays that start from the beginning of the array.

steps.title: Iterate Through Array
steps.detail: For each number `num` in the input array `nums`:

steps.title: Update Current Sum
steps.detail: Add `num` to `currentSum`.

steps.title: Check for Target Sum
